content:
# Philippians Chapter 3 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Philippians Chapter 3 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 3:1 -- "Opening exhortation"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The remaining [thing], my brothers, rejoice in [the] Lord. The same things to write to you, to me [is] not tiresome, but for you [is] safe." | Hyper-literal: preserves TO LOIPON as "the remaining [thing]," renders OKNERON as "tiresome," ASPHALES as "safe." |
| LOGOS | "Finally, my brothers, rejoice in the Lord. To write the same things to you is not troublesome for me, and for you it is a safeguard." | Formal equivalent: TO LOIPON as "Finally," OKNERON as "troublesome," ASPHALES as "a safeguard." |
| PNEUMA | "Finally, my brothers and sisters, find your joy in the Lord! I do not mind writing these same things to you again — it is a safeguard for you." | Dynamic: adds "and sisters," expands "rejoice" to "find your joy," adds "again," restructures sentence. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS rendering. "Finally" is the standard English equivalent for TO LOIPON in epistolary contexts. "Troublesome" and "safeguard" are accurate and natural. PNEUMA's "brothers and sisters" adds gender-inclusive language not in the Greek ADELPHOI. EMET's "tiresome" and "safe" are defensible but less precise than LOGOS.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:2 -- "Triple warning"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Look out for the dogs, look out for the evil workers, look out for the mutilation." | Literal: "look out for" for BLEPETE, retains the noun "the mutilation." |
| LOGOS | "Beware of the dogs, beware of the evil workers, beware of the mutilation." | Formal: "beware of" for BLEPETE, same structure. |
| PNEUMA | "Watch out for those dogs. Watch out for those who do evil. Watch out for those who mutilate the flesh." | Dynamic: adds demonstrative "those," expands "evil workers" to "those who do evil," expands "mutilation" to "those who mutilate the flesh." Separate sentences with periods. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Beware of" is more formally weighted than "look out for" (EMET) or "watch out for" (PNEUMA). PNEUMA's expansion of KATATOMHN into "those who mutilate the flesh" interprets the noun as a verbal phrase, losing the terse wordplay with PERITOMH in v. 3. The demonstrative "those" is an addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:3 -- "The true circumcision"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For we are the circumcision, the [ones] by [the] Spirit of God worshipping and boasting in Christ Jesus and not in flesh having confidence" | Literal: follows Greek word order closely, preserves participial constructions. |
| LOGOS | "For we are the circumcision, who worship by the Spirit of God and boast in Christ Jesus and place no confidence in the flesh—" | Formal: relative clause restructuring, natural English word order. |
| PNEUMA | "For it is we who are the true circumcision — we who worship by the Spirit of God, who place our confidence in Christ Jesus, and who put no trust in outward credentials." | Dynamic: adds "true," adds "it is we who" for emphasis, renders SARX as "outward credentials," restructures into three parallel "who" clauses. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "true" is an interpretive addition not in the Greek. "Outward credentials" for SARKI is interpretive — "flesh" is the literal term and carries the full Pauline theological weight. EMET's word order is too Greek for English readability. LOGOS preserves the participial force with natural syntax.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:4 -- "Confidence in the flesh"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "although I [am] having confidence also in flesh. If any other [man] thinks to have confidence in flesh, I more:" | Literal: "although," bracketed "[am]" and "[man]," preserves Greek structure. |
| LOGOS | "though I myself have reason for confidence even in the flesh. If anyone else thinks to have confidence in the flesh, I more so:" | Formal: adds "reason for," "myself" for emphasis, "even" for KAI, "more so." |
| PNEUMA | "Though if anyone thinks they have reason to trust in such things, I have even more:" | Dynamic: collapses vv. 4-6 into a continuous paragraph, "trust in such things" for "confidence in flesh," uses "they" as singular pronoun. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Reason for confidence" helpfully unpacks the elliptical Greek. "I myself" renders the emphatic EGW. PNEUMA's collapsing of vv. 4-6 into a single flowing paragraph is a structural departure; the consensus maintains verse-by-verse formatting. EMET's bracketed supplements are too apparatus-like.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:5 -- "Paul's credentials"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "circumcision on [the] eighth day, of [the] race of Israel, of [the] tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews, according to [the] law a Pharisee," | Literal: "race" for GENOUS, preserves asyndetic list structure. |
| LOGOS | "circumcised on the eighth day, of the nation of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew of Hebrews; according to the law, a Pharisee;" | Formal: "circumcised" (participial form), "nation" for GENOUS, adds semicolons for structural clarity. |
| PNEUMA | "circumcised on the eighth day, born into the people of Israel, from the tribe of Benjamin, a Hebrew through and through. When it came to the Law, I was a Pharisee." | Dynamic: "born into the people of Israel," "a Hebrew through and through" for EBRAIOS EX EBRAIWN, "When it came to" restructuring. Full sentences. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Nation" for GENOUS is appropriate; EMET's "race" is possible but carries modern connotations. PNEUMA's "born into the people of Israel" adds "born into" not in the Greek. "A Hebrew through and through" loses the literalness of the EX EBRAIWN construction. LOGOS preserves the staccato list structure of the Greek credentials.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:6 -- "Zeal and righteousness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to zeal persecuting the assembly, according to righteousness the [one] in [the] law having become blameless." | Literal: "assembly" for EKKLHSIAN, follows Greek participial structure. |
| LOGOS | "according to zeal, persecuting the church; according to the righteousness that is in the law, having become blameless." | Formal: "church" for EKKLHSIAN, adds relative clause "that is in." |
| PNEUMA | "When it came to zeal, I persecuted the church. When it came to the righteousness the Law demands, I was faultless." | Dynamic: "When it came to" restructuring, "the Law demands" is interpretive, "faultless" for AMEMPTOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Church" is the standard rendering of EKKLHSIA in NT context. PNEUMA's "the Law demands" adds interpretive content; "faultless" is a near-synonym of "blameless" but AMEMPTOS is traditionally rendered "blameless." EMET's "assembly" is defensible but less conventional.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:7 -- "Gains counted as loss"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But whatever things were to me gains, these I have counted on account of Christ [as] loss." | Literal: "to me gains" follows Greek dative, brackets "[as]." |
| LOGOS | "But whatever things were gain to me, these I have counted as loss on account of Christ." | Formal: natural English word order, "gain" singular. |
| PNEUMA | "But everything I once counted as an asset, I have now written off as a loss because of Christ." | Dynamic: "asset" for KERDH, "written off" captures the accounting metaphor, adds "once" and "now" for temporal contrast. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. PNEUMA's "asset" and "written off" are vivid but interpretive — KERDH is simply "gains" and HGHMAI is "counted/considered," not specifically an accounting write-off. EMET's word order ("to me gains") is too Greek. LOGOS preserves the accounting metaphor without over-interpretation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:8 -- "The surpassing worth of knowing Christ"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But indeed also I count all things to be loss on account of the surpassing [thing] of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord, on account of whom the all things I suffered loss, and I count [them] to be refuse, in order that Christ I might gain" | Literal: "surpassing [thing]," "the all things," retains Greek word order for "Christ I might gain." |
| LOGOS | "Indeed, I even count all things to be loss on account of the surpassing worth of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord, on account of whom I suffered the loss of all things, and I count them as refuse, that I may gain Christ" | Formal: supplies "worth" for TO UPERECHON, natural word order. |
| PNEUMA | "More than that, I consider everything to be a loss compared to the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord. For his sake I have thrown it all away — I count it as garbage — so that I may gain Christ" | Dynamic: "knowing" instead of "the knowledge of," "thrown it all away" for EZHMIWQHN, "garbage" for SKUBALA, em-dash parenthetical structure. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS with minor adjustments. "Surpassing worth" helpfully renders the substantive participle TO UPERECHON. "Refuse" for SKUBALA preserves the visceral force in formal register — PNEUMA's "garbage" is colloquial. PNEUMA's "thrown it all away" adds interpretive force beyond EZHMIWQHN ("I suffered the loss of"). EMET's word order is too Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:9 -- "Righteousness through faith"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "and might be found in him, not having my righteousness the [one] from [the] law, but the [one] through faith of Christ, the righteousness the [one] from God upon the faith," | Literal: "faith of Christ" (objective or subjective genitive left ambiguous), "upon the faith" for EPI THI PISTEI. |
| LOGOS | "and be found in him, not having my own righteousness which is from the law, but that which is through faith in Christ, the righteousness from God upon the basis of faith," | Formal: "faith in Christ" (objective genitive interpretation), "upon the basis of faith" for EPI THI PISTEI. |
| PNEUMA | "and be found belonging to him, not holding a righteousness of my own that comes from keeping the Law, but the righteousness that comes through trusting in Christ — the righteousness from God that rests on faith." | Dynamic: "belonging to him," "keeping the Law," "trusting in Christ," "rests on faith." Extensive interpretive expansion. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a mediating rendering. "Faith in Christ" follows LOGOS's objective genitive interpretation, which is the majority reading. For EPI THI PISTEI, "that rests upon faith" was chosen — drawing from PNEUMA's "rests on" (which captures the dative-locative force) while preserving LOGOS's precision. PNEUMA's "belonging to him" adds meaning beyond EN AUTW ("in him"); "keeping the Law" interprets EK NOMOU beyond what the Greek states.

**Status:** Resolved as a mediating blend. LOGOS base with PNEUMA's "rests upon" for the EPI THI PISTEI clause.

---

## 3:10 -- "Knowing Christ's power and sufferings"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the to know him and the power of the resurrection of him and the fellowship of the sufferings of him, being conformed to the death of him," | Literal: articular infinitive "the to know," genitive chains "of the resurrection of him." |
| LOGOS | "that I may know him and the power of his resurrection and the fellowship of his sufferings, being conformed to his death," | Formal: purpose clause "that I may know," possessive pronouns. |
| PNEUMA | "My goal is to know him — to experience the power of his resurrection and to share in his sufferings, being shaped into the pattern of his death," | Dynamic: "My goal is to know him," "experience" for power, "share in" for fellowship, "shaped into the pattern of" for SUMMORFIZOMENOS. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "That I may know" correctly renders the articular infinitive TOU GNWNAI as a purpose clause. PNEUMA's "experience" and "share in" are interpretive expansions of "know" and "fellowship." "Shaped into the pattern of" over-translates SUMMORFIZOMENOS — "being conformed to" is more precise. EMET's genitive chains are unreadable in English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:11 -- "Attaining the resurrection"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if somehow I might attain to the resurrection the [one] from [the] dead." | Literal: "might attain" (subjunctive), "the [one] from [the] dead." |
| LOGOS | "if somehow I may attain to the resurrection from the dead." | Formal: "may attain" (subjunctive). |
| PNEUMA | "so that somehow I may arrive at the resurrection from the dead." | Dynamic: "so that" for EI PWS, "arrive at" for KATANTHSW. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. EI PWS is "if somehow" — a conditional particle expressing humility, not purpose. PNEUMA's "so that" converts the conditional to a purpose clause, subtly changing the theological nuance. "Attain" is more precise than "arrive at" for KATANTAW in this eschatological context. All three preserve the tentative tone.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:12 -- "Pressing on toward perfection"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Not that already I received or already have been perfected, but I pursue if also I might lay hold, for which also I was laid hold of by Christ Jesus." | Literal: "I received" (no object), "perfected," "I pursue," "lay hold." |
| LOGOS | "Not that I have already obtained this or have already been made perfect, but I press on, that I may also lay hold of that for which I was also laid hold of by Christ Jesus." | Formal: supplies "this," "made perfect," "press on," adds "that" (relative pronoun). |
| PNEUMA | "Not that I have already reached this goal or have already been made complete. But I press on to take hold of it, because Christ Jesus has already taken hold of me." | Dynamic: "reached this goal," "made complete," "take hold of it," "because Christ Jesus has already taken hold of me" — restructures the passive with active voice. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Made perfect" for TETELEIWMAI preserves the traditional rendering. PNEUMA's "made complete" is a valid alternative but "perfect" carries the weight of the TELEIOS word group which recurs in v. 15. PNEUMA's restructuring from passive ("I was laid hold of by Christ Jesus") to active ("Christ Jesus has already taken hold of me") is clear but departs from the Greek voice. "Press on" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is better than "pursue" (EMET) for DIWKW in athletic context.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:13 -- "Forgetting and straining forward"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Brothers, I myself do not reckon to have laid hold; but one thing, the things behind forgetting and to the things before stretching out," | Literal: "reckon" for LOGIZOMAI, "stretching out" for EPEKTEINOMENOS, follows Greek syntax. |
| LOGOS | "Brothers, I do not consider myself to have laid hold of it. But one thing I do: forgetting the things that are behind and straining forward to the things that are ahead," | Formal: "consider myself," "straining forward," "things that are ahead." |
| PNEUMA | "Brothers and sisters, I do not consider myself to have seized it yet. But one thing I do: forgetting what lies behind and straining toward what lies ahead," | Dynamic: "brothers and sisters," "seized it" for KATEILHFENAI, "what lies behind/ahead" for more natural English. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Consider" is better than "reckon" (EMET) for LOGIZOMAI in this context. "Straining forward" captures EPEKTEINOMENOS well — EMET's "stretching out" is too physical, PNEUMA's "straining toward" drops "forward." PNEUMA again adds "and sisters." "Seized" (PNEUMA) is too forceful for KATALAMBANW here; "laid hold of" is more traditional.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:14 -- "The prize of the upward calling"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "according to [the] goal I pursue toward the prize of the upward calling of God in Christ Jesus." | Literal: "according to [the] goal," "I pursue" for DIWKW. |
| LOGOS | "I press on toward the goal for the prize of the upward calling of God in Christ Jesus." | Formal: "press on toward the goal for the prize." |
| PNEUMA | "I press on toward the finish line to win the prize — God's upward call in Christ Jesus." | Dynamic: "finish line" for SKOPON, "to win the prize," "God's upward call" restructured with possessive. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. SKOPON means "goal" or "mark" — PNEUMA's "finish line" is an interpretive expansion drawing on athletic imagery that, while apt, goes beyond the Greek term. "Press on" is better than "pursue" (EMET) in the athletic metaphor. KATA SKOPON DIWKW is "I press on toward the goal" — EMET's "according to [the] goal" is too literal for KATA here.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:15 -- "The mature mindset"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "As many therefore [as are] perfect, this let us think; and if anything differently you think, also this God will reveal to you;" | Literal: "perfect" for TELEIOI, "let us think" for FRONWMEN, follows Greek word order. |
| LOGOS | "Therefore, as many of us as are perfect, let us think this way; and if in anything you think differently, God will reveal this also to you." | Formal: "perfect," "let us think this way." |
| PNEUMA | "So let all of us who are mature think this way. And if on some point you think differently, God will make that clear to you as well." | Dynamic: "mature" for TELEIOI, "make that clear" for APOKALUYEI, "on some point" for EI TI. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a mediating rendering using "mature" (PNEUMA) rather than "perfect" (EMET/LOGOS). TELEIOI in this context refers to spiritual maturity rather than sinless perfection — Paul has just said in v. 12 that he has not been "perfected" (TETELEIWMAI). Using "perfect" here creates a seeming contradiction; "mature" captures the intended sense. Otherwise LOGOS's structure is followed. "Reveal" is retained over PNEUMA's "make clear" as it preserves the theological weight of APOKALUPTW.

**Status:** Resolved as a mediating blend. PNEUMA's "mature" with LOGOS's structure.

---

## 3:16 -- "Walking by the same standard"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "nevertheless, to what we have attained, by the same to walk." | Literal: preserves the elliptical Greek, infinitive "to walk." |
| LOGOS | "Nevertheless, to what we have attained, let us walk by the same rule." | Formal: adds hortatory "let us," supplies "rule" for implied KANONI. |
| PNEUMA | "Only let us hold true to what we have already reached." | Dynamic: completely restructured, "hold true" for STOICHEIN, "already reached" for EFQASAMEN. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a mediating rendering based on LOGOS but with "standard" instead of "rule." LOGOS's "let us walk" helpfully converts the infinitive STOICHEIN to a hortatory subjunctive. "Rule" (KANONI) is present in some manuscripts but the Sinaiticus shorter reading may not include it; "standard" is a compromise that conveys the sense without relying on a textual variant. PNEUMA's total restructuring loses the walking metaphor (STOICHEIN = walk in line). EMET's infinitive construction is too elliptical.

**Status:** Resolved as a mediating blend. LOGOS base with "standard" for "rule."

---

## 3:17 -- "Following the pattern"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Fellow imitators of me become, brothers, and observe the [ones] thus walking, just as you have us [as] a pattern." | Literal: "Fellow imitators of me become" follows Greek word order, "pattern" for TUPON. |
| LOGOS | "Become fellow imitators of me, brothers, and observe those who walk according to the pattern you have in us." | Formal: natural word order, "according to the pattern you have in us." |
| PNEUMA | "Join together in following my example, brothers and sisters, and keep your eyes on those who live according to the pattern we have set for you." | Dynamic: "following my example" for SUMMIMHTAI, "keep your eyes on," "live" for PERIPATOUNTAS, "we have set for you" — reverses the direction of the pattern. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "Become fellow imitators of me" preserves SUMMIMHTAI MOU faithfully. PNEUMA's "join together in following my example" is a valid paraphrase but loses the compound SUMMIMHTAI ("co-imitators"). "We have set for you" reverses the Greek — the pattern is something the Philippians "have in us" (ECHETE TUPON HMAS), not something Paul "set for" them. EMET's word order is too Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:18 -- "Enemies of the cross"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For many walk, of whom often I was telling you, and now also weeping I say, the enemies of the cross of Christ," | Literal: "I was telling" (imperfect), "weeping I say" preserves participial order. |
| LOGOS | "For many walk, of whom I told you often, and now tell you even weeping, as the enemies of the cross of Christ," | Formal: "I told you often," "even weeping," adds "as" before "the enemies." |
| PNEUMA | "For many live as enemies of the cross of Christ — I have told you about them often, and now I say it again with tears." | Dynamic: restructures entirely — moves "enemies of the cross" forward, "with tears" for KLAIWN, adds "again." Parenthetical dash. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The word order preserving the participial "weeping" in close proximity to "I tell you" is important — Paul's tears are the mode of his present speech, not an afterthought. PNEUMA's restructuring moves the weeping to the end, weakening the rhetorical impact. "Even weeping" (LOGOS) is better than "also weeping" (EMET). LOGOS's "as" before "enemies" smooths the appositional construction.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:19 -- "Their end is destruction"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whose the end [is] destruction, whose the god [is] the belly, and the glory in the shame of them, the [ones] the earthly things thinking." | Literal: follows Greek word order closely, "the [ones] the earthly things thinking" for the participle. |
| LOGOS | "whose end is destruction, whose god is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame, who set their minds on earthly things." | Formal: adds "whose" for parallel structure, "who set their minds on" for FRONOUNTES. |
| PNEUMA | "Their end is destruction. Their god is their appetite. Their glory is in their shame. Their minds are set on earthly things." | Dynamic: four short declarative sentences, "appetite" for KOILIA, "minds are set" — passive restructuring. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The relative clause chain ("whose...whose...whose...who") faithfully mirrors the Greek participial and genitive constructions. PNEUMA's four staccato sentences are rhetorically powerful but break the syntactic connection to the antecedent in v. 18. "Belly" is retained over PNEUMA's "appetite" — KOILIA literally means belly/stomach, and "appetite" is already interpretive. EMET's word order is too Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:20 -- "Citizenship in heaven"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For our citizenship in heavens exists, from which also [a] savior we eagerly await, [the] Lord Jesus Christ," | Literal: "in heavens" (plural), "exists" for UPARCHEI, "[a] savior" with article bracket, word order "savior we eagerly await." |
| LOGOS | "For our citizenship exists in heaven, from which also we eagerly await a Savior, the Lord Jesus Christ," | Formal: "exists in heaven" (singular), natural word order. |
| PNEUMA | "But our citizenship is in heaven, and it is from there that we eagerly await a Savior — the Lord Jesus Christ." | Dynamic: "But" instead of "For," "is" for UPARCHEI, "it is from there that" — cleft sentence, em-dash before title. |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted a mediating rendering: "For our citizenship is in heaven" — using "For" (GAR, with EMET/LOGOS) and "is" (with PNEUMA, since UPARCHEI functions as a copula here and "exists" is overly emphatic). PNEUMA's "But" misrenders GAR. "From which" is retained over PNEUMA's "from there" as it preserves the relative pronoun EX OU. EMET's "heavens" (plural OURANOIS) is literally correct but "heaven" is standard English usage.

**Status:** Resolved as a mediating blend. "Is" from PNEUMA, "For" and structure from LOGOS.

---

## 3:21 -- "The transformation of the body"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who will transform the body of the humiliation of us [to be] conformed to the body of the glory of him, according to the working of the to be able him also to subject to himself the all things." | Literal: genitive chains, "the to be able him" for articular infinitive, "the all things." |
| LOGOS | "who will transform the body of our humiliation into conformity with the body of his glory, according to the working of his power even to subject all things to himself." | Formal: "into conformity with," "his power" for THN ENERGEIAN TOU DUNASQAI AUTON, "even to subject." |
| PNEUMA | "He will transform our lowly bodies to be like his glorious body, by the power that enables him to bring everything under his authority." | Dynamic: "lowly bodies" (plural) for TO SWMA THS TAPEINWSEWS, "glorious body," "the power that enables him," "bring everything under his authority." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. "The body of our humiliation" preserves the genitive construction that carries theological weight — it is not merely a "lowly body" (PNEUMA) but a body characterized by the present state of humiliation. PNEUMA pluralizes "bodies" where the Greek has singular TO SWMA. "Into conformity with the body of his glory" is precise for SUMMORFON TW SWMATI THS DOXHS AUTOU. LOGOS's "his power" economically renders the complex articular infinitive construction. EMET's "the to be able him" is unreadable English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

— SOPHIA
